## Issue 40733525

Chromium > Platform > Extensions

40733525

### ServiceWorker is shut down every 5 minutes for manifest V3 extension 254 Hotlists (2) Mark as Duplicate

Comments (99+) Dependencies (0) Duplicates (0) Blocking Resources (2)

Fixed

Feature Request P2

Add Hotlist ExtensionServiceWorker Triaged-ET #### Status Update

No update yet. #### Description

[Deleted User] created issue \

Nov 24, 2020 04:33AM

UserAgent: Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10\_15\_6) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/86.0.4240.198 Safari/537.36
Steps to reproduce the problem:  
1\. Create a manifest V3 extension with ServiceWorker  
2\. Create WebSocket connection form ServiceWorker  
3\. Create "runtime.Port" connection from ContentScript to ServiceWorker  
4\. Send messages from ContentScript to ServiceWorker to WebSocket and vice versa with some rate.  
  
What is the expected behavior?  
ServiceWorker stays alive due to active messaging.  
  
What went wrong?  
ServiceWorker is forcefully shut down every 5 minutes.  
  
Did this work before? N/A  
  
Does this work in other browsers? N/A  
  
Chrome version: 89.0.4334.0  Channel: n/a  
OS Version: OS X 10.15.6  
Flash Version:  
  
Test repository where the issue can be reproduced <https://github.com/maikudou/chrome-service-worker>  
  
5 minutes delay was also mentioned in comments for this issue, but slightly different context. <https://bugs.chromium.org/p/chromium/issues/detail?id=1146434>  
Therefore, I've created a new bug report. ### Issue summary

Links (2)

# Chrome extension MV3: persistent service worker die after wake up from hibernation

Ask Question

Asked 2 years ago

Modified 2 years ago

Viewed 1k times

1
I'm using this solution by wOxxOm [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/66618136/persistent-service-worker-in-chrome-extension/) to keep the service worker alive in my chrome extension (manifest v3).

I used the offscreen solution because I use win7 so I can only use chrome v109. it works very well, but when the PC hibernate then wake up, the service worker will restart with a probability of 50%. for example if I hibernate after 10s and wake up after 10s then the SW will restart always...

This problem does not happen in my tests when I use background scripts using manifest v2 with `"persistent" : true`

Is there something I can do to make the SW persistent (not restart after hibernation) as in MV2?

I tested other solutions but none worked, for example I discovered that I can make the SW persistent using alarms too:

```
// keep SW alive for 30s
// self.serviceWorker.postMessage('test')
 ...

results.title: Web extension converted from chrome to safari fails with error "The service_worker script failed to load due to an error." - Stack Overflow
excerpts:
Web extension converted from chrome to safari fails with error "The service_worker script failed to load due to an error."
Ask Question
Asked
2 years, 7 months ago
Modified 2 years ago
Viewed 2k times
1
As stated in the title, I am trying to convert a web extension originally made for chrome to safari, using the tool documented at https://developer.apple.com/documentation/safariservices/safari_web_extensions/converting_a_web_extension_for_safari
The project is created, builds, and launches successfully, however when the extension is enabled in safari I get 2 errors:
    * "An extension with a non-persistent background page cannot listen to
webRequest events."
    * "The
service_worker script failed to load due to an error."

The first error is a general bug in chromium, which is fixed in v107 (verified in chrome canary), and the extension relies on this API to work.
I have no idea what to do with the second error, as it provides no information at all.
The option to access the background page process is disabled in the Safari Develop menu with the message "service worker failed to load".
Here is my "manifest.json"
{
"name":"...",
"manifest_version":3,
"content_scripts":[{"all_frames":false,"js":["content.js"],"matches":["file://*/*","http://*/*","https://*/*"],"run_at":"document_idle","match_origin_as_fallback":true}],
"host_permissions":["<all_urls>"],
"permissions":["tabs","activeTab","storage","scripting","notifications","webRequest","downloads","alarms"],
"background":{"service_worker":"background.js"},
"content_security_policy":{"extension_pages":"script-src 'self' 'wasm-unsafe-eval'; object-src 'self'"},
"web_accessible_resources":[{"resources":["inject.js"],"matches":["<all_urls>"]}],
"action":{"default_popup":"popup.html"},
"icons":{"16":"resources/icons/16x16.png","32":"resources/icons/32x32.png","48":"resources/icons/48x48.png","128":"resources/icons/128x128.png"},

results.title: javascript - Persistent Service Worker in Chrome Extension - Stack Overflow
excerpts:
I need to define my Service Worker as persistent in my Chrome extension because I'm using the webRequest API to intercept some data passed in a form for a specific request, but I don't know how I can do that. I've tried everything, but my Service Worker keeps unloading.

How can I keep it loaded and waiting until the request is intercepted?

* javascript
* google-chrome
* google-chrome-extension
* request
* xmlhttprequest

Share

Improve this question

Follow

asked Mar 13, 2021 at 20:16

Keven Augusto

1,235 2 2 gold badges 8 8 silver badges 6 6 bronze badges

Add a comment | 

## 10 Answers 10

Sorted by: Reset to default

Highest score (default) Trending (recent votes count more) Date modified (newest first) Date created (oldest first)

254

#### Table of contents

* Description and known problems
* Workarounds:
  
  • Bug exploit  
  • `offscreen` API  
  • `nativeMessaging` API  
  • `WebSocket` API  
  • `chrome` messaging API  
  • Dedicated tab
* Caution

* * *
Service worker (SW) can't be persistent by definition and the browser must forcibly terminate all its activities/requests after a certain time, which in Chrome is 5 minutes. The inactivity timer (i.e. when no such activities are ongoing) is even shorter: 30 seconds.

Chromium team [currently considers](https://crbug.com/1152255) this behavior good (the team relaxes some aspects though occasionally e.g. Chrome 114 prolongs chrome.runtime port after each message), however this is only good for extensions that observe infrequent events, which run just a few times a day thus reducing browser's memory footprint between the runs (for example, webRequest/webNavigation events with `urls` filter for a rarely visited site). These extensions can be reworked to maintain the state, [example](https://stackoverflow.com/a/73090402) . Unfortunately, such an idyll is unsustainable in many cases:

* Problem: [worker dies in the middle of a long-running `fetch()`](https://crbug.com/40283184) .

# service worker works only when DevTools (Console) is open

Ask Question

Asked 4 years, 8 months ago

Modified 4 years, 8 months ago

Viewed 762 times

2

I'm building chrome extension, and in background script I use:

```
chrome.alarms.create({periodInMinutes: 1})

chrome.alarms.onAlarm.
addListener(() => {
  doSomeStuff();
});
```

I do it few times, and in the last call, I do `clearAll` alarms.

The problem which I encounter in this situation is:
My app works perfectly when Console is open.
But if I close my console in Chrome, my app stops working.

My opinion is that when I open Console to inspect `service worker` service worker is awake, but when I close it my service worker goes into idle (sleep) mode.

How to solve it?

* google-chrome-extension
* service-worker

Share

Improve this question

Follow

edited Jun 29, 2021 at 13:45

Tresor Bireke

113 6 6 bronze badges

asked Jun 29, 2021 at 6:54

Predrag Davidovic

1,586 2 2 gold badges 18 18 silver badges 24 24 bronze badges

3

* 2
  
  The code you've shown isn't a problem. The problem is elsewhere. Service worker lives for 30 seconds so you should use chrome.storage.local to read/write the state in the event listener, not global variables.

# How do you call a function every second in a Chrome Extension Manifest V3 Background Service Worker?

Ask Question

Asked 4 years, 3 months ago

Modified 2 years, 7 months ago
Viewed 3k times

6

Service workers replace background pages in Manifest v3 Google Chrome Extensions, and I’m trying to use one for my extension. The documentation says timers ( `setTimeout` and `setInterval` ) should be avoided because timers get canceled when service workers terminate (which may happen at any time). Unlike background pages, service workers can’t be persistent and will always be subject to termination. The documentation recommends using the _Alarms API_ instead.

I need to be able to run a function periodically in a Manifest v3 service worker, but alarms don’t suit my use case because they’re slow. **The shortest allowed alarm duration is 1 minute, but I want to run a function in shorter intervals—a second or a few seconds.**

I tried using `event.waitUntil` to keep the service worker alive per [this Stack Overflow answer.](https://stackoverflow.

# Troubleshooting your Safari web extension

Use developer resources to investigate and resolve common problems with Safari web extensions.

## Overview

When you experience issues with your Safari web extension, choose the right developer tool or resource to troubleshoot and resolve the issue, based on the type of issue you experience.

### Install and enable the extension in Safari
